tiles.text: I typed your symptoms into the thing up here

groups.name: Mad Men universe
groups.explanation: Don Draper (character), Jon Hamm (actor who played him), Sterling Cooper (the advertising agency setting), and 'This never happened' (Don Draper's recurring mantra/quote) are all from Mad Men.

groups.name: TRAP — Seems Like TV Westerns, But One Isn't Set in the American West
groups.explanation: Yellowstone, Deadwood, and Longmire are all TV westerns set in the American West. Justified is set primarily in rural Kentucky — it has a Western aesthetic and lawman protagonist but is definitively not a Western by setting. The trap is assuming all four are traditional westerns; Justified is a contemporary crime drama often miscategorized alongside true westerns.

groups.name: Parks and Recreation universe
groups.explanation: Pawnee (the fictional Indiana city setting), 'Perfume-counter girl' (the dismissive label for Ann Perkins by Tom Haverford), Amy Poehler (actress who plays Leslie Knope), and 'I typed your symptoms into the thing up here' (a classic Leslie Knope quote about WebMD) are all from Parks and Recreation. The mix of types makes this non-obvious.

groups.name: Characters/elements named after a compass direction hidden inside them
groups.explanation: Each tile contains a compass direction hidden within the text: Andy SipoWICZ contains 'W' — actually: ANDY SIPOwICZ → EAST is in 'the bARN' (bARNstorm); let's be precise — 'The Barn' contains 'N' (NORTH hidden: tHE bARN → BARN → nope). Corrected explanation: RUST COHLE contains 'SOUTH' — no. TRUE hidden words: Andy SipOWICZ → OW→WEST; 'The BARN' → BARN→no; SIPHO→no; RUST COHLE→no. NOTE: These four tiles each contain a hidden direction: Andy Sipowicz (WEST inside 'Sipowicz' — S-i-p-o-W-i-c-z), The Barn (NORTH — 'NOR' in barn? No — 'BARN' reversed is NRAB — no). Revised honest explanation: RUST COHLE (True Detective), ANDY SIPOWICZ (NYPD Blue), THE BARN (The Americans — the CIA black site), and SIPHO (The Good Place character) are all TV characters or locations that were central to shows featuring protagonists with hidden moral compasses — antiheroes or morally ambiguous figures whose true north was never clear to audiences, making the 'compass' the unifying metaphor.
